subject: English
overall_learning_objectives: Understand the life and perspective of Helen Keller
Identify the things Helen Keller wishes to see if given sight
Learn the value of appreciating our five senses
Recognize the importance of gratitude for everyday things
Develop basic descriptive vocabulary and reading fluency
Understand the sequence of her 'three-day plan'

lesson_plan.title: Who is Helen Keller?
topics_covered: The concept of 'seeing' through touch and heart
teaching_strategy: Class discussion on how we use our senses
Introductory talk on Helen Keller’s achievements
Sensory activity: Identifying objects while blindfolded
student_activities: Share how they would feel if they couldn't see for a day
Brainstorm a list of favorite things they see every day

lesson_plan.title: The First Day: People and Home
topics_covered: Helen’s desire to see her friends and teacher (Anne Sullivan)
Appreciating the faces of loved ones
Seeing the small items that make a home
teaching_strategy: Guided reading of the 'First Day' section
Discussion on why faces are important
student_activities: Draw a picture of a person who is important to them

lesson_plan.title: The Second Day: History and Nature
topics_covered: Seeing the progress of human history
teaching_strategy: Using pictures of museums to explain her interests
student_activities: List three things in nature they often ignore but are beautiful
Act out a silent scene (miming) to show understanding of visual stories

lesson_plan.title: The Third Day: The Busy World
topics_covered: Seeing people in their daily routines
The realization that time is short
teaching_strategy: Discussion on the 'hustle and bustle' of a city
Reading the final part of the essay
Highlighting the lesson: 'Use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind'
student_activities: Write a list of 5 places in their city they would show Helen
Discuss in groups: Why does Helen want to see 'work'?

lesson_plan.title: Gratitude and Senses
topics_covered: Connecting the story to our own habits
teaching_strategy: Creating a 'Gratitude Tree' on the board
Reading Helen's final advice to the readers
Sharing stories of things we take for granted
student_activities: Write a 'Thank You' note to their eyes/ears
Fill in a 'Three Days' summary table

lesson_plan.title: Vocabulary and Revision
teaching_strategy: Revision quiz on Helen’s three-day itinerary
Sentence building exercise using 'I would see...'

teaching_aids: Images of Helen Keller and Anne Sullivan
teacher_tip: Remind students that Helen Keller isn't asking for big miracles—she wants to see 'simple' things. Encourage them to walk home today and find one small thing they usually ignore but find beautiful.
